context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on this climate action plan thing rn, can u just explain the whole prospectus from the start?
exchanges.tutor: Let's figure out where you actually are first! Have you already chosen your case study city and completed your SWOT Analysis? That's the foundation for this prospectus, so knowing where you stand will help us figure out the right starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the assignment, which would be too much scaffolding and essentially doing the work for them. Instead, I should ask a targeted question to figure out where exactly they are stuck and what they already know, so I can guide them from there. I'll also check if they've done any prior work (the SWOT analysis mentioned in the assignment) since this is a scaffolded course.

exchanges.student: umm kinda? i picked a city i think, but tbh i'm confused about like all the parts and how they fit together.
exchanges.tutor: Good, having a city is a solid start! What city did you pick? And in your own words, what do you think this assignment is asking you to produce — even if you're not 100% sure?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a city but is confused about the assignment structure. Rather than explaining everything at once, I should narrow down what they're confused about. The assignment has a clear structure (2 components, each with Context, Opportunity, Policy Recommendation, EJ Implications, Interactions), so I should get them to engage with the structure themselves rather than just handing it to them. I'll ask them to tell me what city they picked and what they think the assignment is asking them to do, to gauge their understanding.

exchanges.student: i picked miami i think, but ngl i don't really get the assignment at all, like can u just walk thru the whole thing from beginning to end?
exchanges.tutor: Miami is a great pick — it's highly relevant to climate action! I'm not going to walk through the whole thing for you, but I will help you figure it out step by step. So let's start simple: what do you think is the biggest climate challenge Miami is facing right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to walk through the whole assignment, which I should not do. Miami is a great choice for climate vulnerability. I need to resist giving a full walkthrough and instead use Socratic method to get them engaged. I'll acknowledge the city choice positively and narrow the focus to the first piece of the assignment structure - asking them what they think 'context' means in this assignment to get them thinking about it themselves.

exchanges.student: maybe sea level rise? but also heat and storms, so now i'm confused again lol.
exchanges.tutor: Those are all real and well-documented challenges for Miami — good instincts! But here's the thing: you only need to write 2 components for this assignment. So which two of those issues feel most important or interesting to you to focus on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually has good instincts here - they've identified real climate vulnerabilities for Miami. I should affirm this and redirect them to focus, since the assignment only requires 2 components. I want them to start making a decision about which two issues to focus on, which is a key part of the assignment. I'll push them to choose rather than staying confused.

exchanges.student: idkkk that's what i'm stuck on, they all seem connected fr. can u just explain how ppl usually pick the 2 components?
exchanges.tutor: You actually just said something really important — that the issues feel connected. Look at the assignment structure: one of the required sections for each component is called 'Interactions,' which is specifically about how your components connect to each other. So the connection you're sensing is a feature, not a problem. Given that, which two of your three issues do you think have the strongest link to each other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to make the decision for them or give them a formula. I should not do that. However, they did make a good observation that the issues are connected - this actually relates directly to the 'Interactions' section of the assignment, which asks about synergies between components. I can use this insight to push them forward without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: maybe heat + flooding? or storms + flooding? tbh i still want like a simple overview of what each section is supposed to do.
exchanges.tutor: Both are solid combinations! But you need to commit — which pair feels more interesting to you personally? Also, the assignment description you have already outlines what each section does (Context, Opportunity, Policy Recommendation, EJ Implications, Interactions). Try reading through it again and tell me what you think one of those sections is asking for. Which one seems clearest to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress on choosing their two components - both heat+flooding and storms+flooding are valid combinations. I should push them to commit to one pair rather than hovering. They're also again asking for a walkthrough of the sections, which I should redirect - they have the assignment description available to them and I shouldn't do that work for them. I'll acknowledge their good thinking and push them to commit to a choice first.

exchanges.student: ugh maybe context? i think it's like what's happening rn, but i'm still super fuzzy on the difference between context and opportunity.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with context! Now think about it this way: if Miami is already experiencing flooding today, what's the difference between describing that problem and identifying something the city could actually *do* about it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable grasp of 'context' and is asking about the distinction between context and opportunity. This is a good and specific question worth engaging with. Rather than explaining the difference directly, I should ask them a question that helps them discover it themselves. I'll use their Miami example to make it concrete.

exchanges.student: i mean context is the problem, and opportunity is maybe the opening to fix it? but can u explain that difference more plainly bc i'm not confident at all.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid distinction — trust yourself on that one! Now apply it: what's one specific opening or opportunity Miami has to address flooding? Think about things like policy, technology, geography, or resources the city might be able to leverage.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually articulated the distinction quite well - context is the problem, opportunity is the opening to act. I should affirm this and not over-explain, as doing so would undermine their confidence in their own correct reasoning. I should push them to apply this to their specific case rather than explaining it more abstractly.

exchanges.student: idk maybe infrastructure stuff? i'm still lost tbh, can u give me a really basic template for one component?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you a template — that's your job to write! But you're actually making more progress than you think. 'Infrastructure' is a real lead. Can you be more specific? What kind of infrastructure in Miami could help with flooding?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a template, which would essentially be doing the work for them. I should not provide one. However, 'infrastructure' is a promising lead worth developing. The student has been going in circles a bit - they have the right instincts (context=problem, opportunity=opening, infrastructure as an opportunity) but keeps second-guessing themselves and asking for more scaffolding. I should push them to be more specific about infrastructure rather than retreating to a template request. I need to be a bit more firm here.

exchanges.student: maybe drainage or pumps i guess, but i'm still confused about how detailed the 200-300 words part is supposed to be.
exchanges.tutor: Drainage and pump systems are exactly the kind of specific infrastructure worth writing about — Miami actually has a real pump network already in place. You now have enough to start: a city, a topic (flooding), a context (sea level rise/flooding problem), and an opportunity (drainage/pump infrastructure). Rather than worrying more about word count, why not try drafting just the Context section first and share it here? That's the best way to figure out the right level of detail.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now identified drainage/pumps as specific infrastructure - this is good concrete progress. They're asking about the level of detail for 200-300 words, which is a reasonable question but also one they can partially answer themselves by thinking about what they already know. We've now had several exchanges and the student has enough pieces to start writing. I should push them toward attempting a first draft rather than continuing to discuss structure. It's time to consolidate what we've discussed and get them writing.
